key_insight: A simple surprise signal from a frozen encoder can gate when new information is written and also serve as a basis for metacognitive responses in both memory and vision-language systems.
engineering_relevance: This suggests a practical control mechanism for continual learning and interactive AI systems, where plasticity and user-facing uncertainty can be triggered by prediction error rather than fixed heuristics.
method: Compute surprise with a small predictor over latent features from a frozen backbone.
Write new episodic memories only when surprise is high, then consolidate them in periodic offline replay into a slow linear readout.
Reuse the same surprise signal in a VLM to choose between confident answers, hedged answers, or refusal plus clarification request.
evidence_signal: On 1000-class continual ImageNet streams, offline consolidation improved retention of the oldest classes by 17.7 points with DINOv2 and 51.3 points with I-JEPA, and the text-image surprise detector achieved AUROC 0.966 for known-vs-novel concepts.
watch_item: Results are proof-of-concept and single-seed in key settings, so the reported gains may be sensitive to backbone choice, replay schedule, and evaluation protocol.
paper_title: Surprise as a Signal for Plasticity and Metacognition
